items.rationale: His flagship project; sustained, accelerating performance milestones (6x speedup, GPU proving at ~1.6B cycles/sec, RV64 support, falling memory) and real adoption (LayerZero).

citations.quote: LayerZero is launching a new blockchain and on the SNARK-proving front it's powered by Jolt.
Jolt just got a 6× speedup. We now prove over 1,000,000 RISC-V cycles/sec on a 32-core CPU

items.rationale: His central design thesis: sum-check over univariate/quotient-based approaches is the key dichotomy for fast SNARK provers; he wrote the canonical survey and keeps shipping optimizations.

citations.quote: New survey: Sum-check is all you need.
The key performance dichotomy in SNARK design... It's the sum-check protocol and multilinear polynomials vs. univariate polynomials.
All the sum-check optimizations we could come up with... ~11x faster, ~17x less memory for key subroutines

items.rationale: Most recent roadmap priority: switching Jolt from elliptic-curve to lattice commitments for ~10x throughput, 128-bit fields, and post-quantum security as a bonus.
items.age_note: Newest strategic direction (2026-02), supersedes earlier elliptic-curve-centric framing in 2025.

citations.quote: switching from elliptic-curve commitments to lattice-based ones—enabling 128-bit fields (vs 256-bit) and post-quantum security to boot.

items.rationale: New memory-checking primitives delivering ~3x end-to-end prover speedups, simpler than Lasso/Spice, validating Jolt's design bets and benefiting non-zkVM SNARKs.

citations.quote: We just unveiled Twist and Shout, two new memory-checking arguments designed to supercharge the Jolt zkVM.
The speedups largely come from integrating our new Twist and Shout memory-checking arguments. They're faster and simpler than the Lasso and Spice arguments

items.rationale: Just shipped native ZK for Jolt via NovaBlindFold — no recursion or wrapping, marginal proof-size cost — opening on-device privacy applications.

citations.quote: Jolt now supports zero-knowledge... suitable for privacy applications — no SNARK recursion, no "wrapping"... Marginal increase in proof size (~3 KB).

items.rationale: Believes proving is approaching real-time for a single CPU core; expects single-GPU real-time proving and continued throughput scaling.

citations.quote: A bit further out: I expect single-GPU, real-time proving of a single CPU core.
today we're already in the ballpark of real-time proving for a single-core CPU execution.

items.rationale: Expects zkVMs to hit 'Stage 2' (10,000x native overhead) by end of 2026, the threshold where non-blockchain applications become interesting.

citations.quote: By the end of 2026, we should hit "Stage 2": 10,000x prover overhead... that's when things get interesting for applications beyond blockchains.

items.rationale: RV64 now proves faster than prior RV32 results; unlocks broader language support (e.g. Go/Geth) and fewer cycles for many apps.

citations.quote: Jolt now proves RISC-V programs with 64-bit registers (RV64IMAC), at speeds exceeding those we previously reported for 32-bit.

items.rationale: Integrating Nova folding and streaming to keep prover memory under 2 GB for arbitrarily long executions without recursion — a usability 'phase change'.
items.age_note: Stated as 'final barrier'/'current focus' in 2024-11; still on the roadmap as of 2025-10, memory now ~700 MB/million cycles.

citations.quote: integrating Jolt with the Nova folding scheme, which will allow for arbitrarily long RISC-V executions with under 2 GB of prover memory—a phase change
Memory usage keeps dropping too, now at ~700 MB per million cycles.

items.rationale: Argues PQ encryption must ship now due to Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later risk, even though a CRQC is far off; endorses hybrid schemes already deployed in TLS/messaging.

citations.quote: post-quantum (PQ) encryption must ship now, despite costs/risks.
The clearest near-term risk is Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later (HNDL)

items.rationale: Repeatedly warns zkVMs are not ready for complex, high-stakes deployments; bugs are pervasive and formal verification is nascent.

citations.quote: Beware the hype: while SNARKs and zkVMs show immense promise, they're not ready for complex, high-stakes deployments.
we flirt with disaster if we pretend they're ready for prime time.

items.rationale: Holds that for years to come bugs, side-channels, and fault injection are larger risks than quantum computers, across SNARKs and signatures; calls for audits/fuzzing/formal methods.

citations.quote: for years to come, bugs and implementation attacks (side-channels, fault injection) will be larger risks than CRQCs.
above all, bugs remain the bigger near-term risk.

items.rationale: Argues a zkVM can't truly be called secure until verified-verifier stage is reached, which is still several years off; even Zcash (far simpler) had a major bug.

citations.quote: Until we reach Stage 2, we can't really call a zkVM "secure"—and getting there is still likely several years off.
Zcash is about the limit of what I'd trust (absent formal verification)... Of course, even Zcash had a major bug in the past.

items.rationale: Overhead versus native execution was a non-starter for most use cases; a structured 'speed stages' roadmap is needed to cut it by orders of magnitude.
items.age_note: 2025-03 figure was >100,000x; by 2025-08 Jolt reportedly crossed <100,000x (Speed Stage 1), so concern is easing but ongoing toward the 10,000x Stage-2 target.

citations.quote: overheads versus native execution still exceed 100,000×—a non-starter for most use cases.
The Jolt prover work overhead is under 100,000... That meets the threshold for what we call Speed Stage 1

items.rationale: Strongly critical of conflating 'logical qubits' (incl. distance-2 error-detecting codes) with the fault-tolerant logical qubits Shor needs; says this distorts perceptions of CRQC proximity.

citations.quote: "logical qubits" in announcements and roadmaps ≠ logical qubits needed for Shor.
raw qubit counts are misleading: we need thousands of error-corrected logical qubits and huge T-gate budgets—far beyond today.

items.rationale: Concerned that headline benchmarks bundle proof system, engineering, hardware, and hand-tuned precompiles into one number, hiding the true state of fundamental efficiency.

citations.quote: many benchmarks bundle everything—proof system, engineering, hardware boosts, and hand-tuned precompiles—into a single top-line number, obscuring where we really stand.

items.rationale: Opposes designing custom SNARK-friendly VMs; they cater to today's SNARK limits and block reuse of mature tooling, whereas RISC-V is already SNARK-friendly with the right techniques.

citations.quote: Proposals for "SNARK-friendly" VMs at best cater to today's SNARK limits, and prevent reuse of mature tooling.

items.rationale: Notes SNARK zero-knowledge stays secure post-quantum but soundness (forging false proofs) becomes vulnerable after a CRQC — a real but non-urgent, deliberate-migration concern.

citations.quote: What becomes vulnerable post-CRQC is soundness (forging new false proofs), not confidentiality or soundness of past proofs.
A cryptographically relevant quantum computer (CRQC) in the 2020s is highly unlikely. Even mid-2030s is ambitious.

items.rationale: Cited approvingly as a flagship adopter: launching a new chain whose SNARK proving is powered by Jolt — evidence of real production traction, though framed as tech validation not a token call.
items.age_note: Mentioned as a technical adoption milestone, not an investment thesis on the token.

citations.quote: LayerZero is launching a new blockchain and on the SNARK-proving front it's powered by Jolt.

items.rationale: Singled out as roughly the most trustworthy SNARK deployment in production — but trust framed as conditional and bounded by lack of formal verification, so it's cautious endorsement.
items.age_note: Single mention from 2025-03-12, never restated; trust is explicitly qualified ('about the limit of what I'd trust').

citations.quote: Zcash is about the limit of what I'd trust (absent formal verification), and it's still far simpler than any zkVM pipeline

items.rationale: Flags Bitcoin's distinctive post-quantum migration challenges: slow governance, no passive migration, and abandoned supply at quantum-vulnerable addresses requiring years of coordination — though not an imminent threat.

citations.quote: Bitcoin's special challenges: slow governance, no passive migration, and a non-trivial abandoned supply at high-value quantum-vulnerable addresses.
Bitcoin has to take the threat seriously — not because CRQCs are coming before 2030, but because... requires years of coordination.

risk_regime.rationale: Thaler is a research lead, not a market allocator; he expresses no asset-allocation risk-on/risk-off view. His posture is structurally bullish on the long-run trajectory of sum-check-based SNARKs and Jolt (rapid, compounding performance gains, expanding to privacy and non-blockchain uses), while being pointedly risk-averse near-term: zkVMs aren't production-ready, bugs/implementation attacks are the dominant danger, formal verification is years away, and he actively deflates both zkVM and quantum-computing hype. Net: optimistic builder, conservative deployer.

notes: Corpus is ~95% technical cryptography commentary from a builder/researcher perspective; there is essentially no trading, valuation, or macro market view, so 'sectors' are rendered as technology themes and token entries are sparse and inferred (LayerZero/Zcash/Bitcoin appear as technical references, not investment theses) — treat token conviction as low. Recency weighting applied: latest roadmap (2026-02/03) elevates lattice-based commitments, native zero-knowledge, and GPU/real-time proving as the current excitement frontier, partly superseding 2024–2025 elliptic-curve-centric framing. The 'performance overhead' concern is moderating over time (>100,000x in 2025-03 → <100,000x / Stage 1 by 2025-08, targeting Stage 2 / 10,000x by end of 2026). On quantum, his consistent stance across the most recent statements (2025-12) is that a CRQC before 2030 is highly unlikely and that hype/communication errors are a bigger problem than the underlying threat — with bugs framed repeatedly as the overriding near-term risk.
